Frontal_Cortex.summary: The front portion of the brain, responsible for executive functions, decision-making, planning, and motor control.
Frontal_Cortex.function: Houses the prefrontal cortex for higher-order thinking, the motor cortex for voluntary movement, Broca's area for speech production, and the cingulate cortex for emotional regulation and attention.
Frontal_Cortex.location: Anterior portion of the brain, extending from the front of the skull to the central sulcus.
Frontal_Cortex.clinical: Damage can cause personality changes, impaired judgment, difficulty planning, motor weakness, and speech problems. Common sites for tumors and traumatic brain injury effects.

Posterior_Cortex.summary: The back and top portions of the brain handling sensory processing, spatial awareness, and visual perception.
Posterior_Cortex.function: The parietal lobe processes touch, spatial orientation, and mathematical reasoning. The occipital lobe is dedicated to visual processing, from basic features to complex object recognition.
Posterior_Cortex.location: Upper and posterior regions of the brain, behind the central sulcus.
Posterior_Cortex.clinical: Damage can cause sensory deficits, spatial neglect (ignoring one side of space), visual agnosia (inability to recognize objects), and reading difficulties.

Temporal_Cortex.summary: The side portions of the brain critical for memory, language comprehension, and emotional processing.
Temporal_Cortex.function: Contains auditory cortex for hearing, Wernicke's area for language understanding, the hippocampus for memory formation, and the amygdala for emotional responses.
Temporal_Cortex.location: Lateral portions of the brain, beneath the temples, extending inward to medial temporal structures.
Temporal_Cortex.clinical: Damage can cause memory impairment (amnesia), difficulty understanding language, hearing problems, and emotional disturbances. Often affected in Alzheimer's disease and temporal lobe epilepsy.

Subcortical.summary: Deep brain structures beneath the cortex that control movement, relay sensory information, and regulate arousal.
Subcortical.function: The basal ganglia coordinate movement and habit learning. The thalamus relays sensory and motor signals. The brainstem controls vital functions and arousal.
Subcortical.location: Central and deep regions of the brain, surrounded by the cerebral cortex.
Subcortical.clinical: Damage can cause movement disorders (Parkinson's, Huntington's), sensory disturbances, sleep problems, and in severe cases, coma.

Cerebellum.summary: The 'little brain' at the back, essential for motor coordination, balance, and motor learning.
Cerebellum.function: Fine-tunes movements for precision and timing, maintains balance and posture, and is involved in motor learning like riding a bike. Recent research shows roles in cognition and emotion.
Cerebellum.location: Posterior and inferior to the cerebral hemispheres, attached to the brainstem.
Cerebellum.clinical: Damage causes ataxia (uncoordinated movements), tremor, slurred speech, and balance problems. Can be affected by alcohol abuse and certain genetic conditions.

Frontal_Lobe.summary: The largest lobe, spanning the front of the brain, controlling executive function and voluntary movement.
Frontal_Lobe.function: Contains the motor cortex for movement execution, prefrontal cortex for planning and decision-making, and Broca's area for speech production. Enables personality expression and impulse control.
Frontal_Lobe.location: Anterior to the central sulcus, comprising about one-third of the cerebral cortex.
Frontal_Lobe.clinical: Frontal lobe syndrome causes personality changes, poor judgment, and disinhibition. Damage to motor areas causes paralysis; to Broca's area causes speech production deficits.

Parietal_Lobe.summary: The sensory processing hub integrating touch, spatial awareness, and mathematical reasoning.
Parietal_Lobe.function: Primary somatosensory cortex receives touch and proprioception. Association areas integrate sensory information for spatial awareness, attention, and numerical processing.
Parietal_Lobe.location: Superior and posterior to the frontal lobe, above the temporal lobe, anterior to the occipital lobe.
Parietal_Lobe.clinical: Damage can cause sensory loss, spatial neglect, difficulty with mathematics, and problems with hand-eye coordination. Gerstmann syndrome involves finger agnosia and writing difficulties.

Temporal_Lobe.summary: The auditory and semantic processing center, critical for hearing, language, and object recognition.
Temporal_Lobe.function: Contains primary auditory cortex, Wernicke's area for language comprehension, and regions for face and object recognition. Involved in semantic memory and emotional associations.
Temporal_Lobe.location: Lateral portion of each hemisphere, below the lateral fissure, at the level of the temples.
Temporal_Lobe.clinical: Wernicke's aphasia impairs language comprehension. Damage can cause auditory processing deficits, face recognition problems (prosopagnosia), and semantic memory loss.

Occipital_Lobe.summary: The visual processing center at the back of the brain, dedicated entirely to vision.
Occipital_Lobe.function: Primary visual cortex receives input from the eyes. Higher visual areas process color, motion, form, and object recognition through dorsal and ventral streams.
Occipital_Lobe.location: Most posterior portion of the brain, above the cerebellum.
Occipital_Lobe.clinical: Damage causes visual field deficits (cortical blindness) or higher visual processing problems like motion blindness or color blindness of central origin.

Cingulate_Cortex.summary: A limbic structure wrapping around the corpus callosum, involved in emotion, attention, and error detection.
Cingulate_Cortex.function: Anterior cingulate monitors errors and conflicts, regulates emotion, and drives motivation. Posterior cingulate is part of the default mode network, involved in self-reflection and memory retrieval.
Cingulate_Cortex.location: Medial surface of each hemisphere, arching over the corpus callosum.
Cingulate_Cortex.clinical: Dysfunction linked to depression, OCD, and ADHD. Damage can cause apathy, emotional blunting, and attention deficits.

Insula.summary: The 'hidden fifth lobe' deep within the lateral sulcus, integrating bodily sensations with emotions.
Insula.function: Processes interoception (awareness of internal body states), taste, disgust, empathy, and self-awareness. Critical for emotional experience and decision-making.
Insula.location: Deep within the lateral fissure, covered by the frontal, parietal, and temporal opercula.
Insula.clinical: Involved in addiction, anxiety disorders, and eating disorders. Damage can impair taste, emotional processing, and body awareness.

Medial_Temporal.summary: Deep temporal structures essential for memory formation and emotional processing.
Medial_Temporal.function: The hippocampus converts short-term to long-term memories. The amygdala processes emotional significance, especially fear. Together they link emotions to memories.
Medial_Temporal.location: Medial aspect of the temporal lobe, deep to the temporal cortex.
Medial_Temporal.clinical: Hippocampal damage causes amnesia. Amygdala dysfunction is linked to anxiety disorders and PTSD. Both are affected early in Alzheimer's disease.

Basal_Ganglia.summary: Deep nuclei critical for movement initiation, habit learning, and reward processing.
Basal_Ganglia.function: The striatum (caudate and putamen) receives cortical input. The pallidum provides output. Together they facilitate wanted movements and inhibit unwanted ones. Also involved in learning habits and processing rewards.
Basal_Ganglia.location: Deep within the cerebral hemispheres, lateral to the thalamus.
Basal_Ganglia.clinical: Degeneration causes Parkinson's disease (too little movement) or Huntington's disease (too much movement). Involved in OCD, Tourette's, and addiction.

Thalamus.summary: The brain's relay station, routing sensory and motor information to appropriate cortical areas.
Thalamus.function: Nearly all sensory information (except smell) passes through thalamic nuclei before reaching cortex. Also relays motor signals and is involved in consciousness and sleep.
Thalamus.location: Central brain structure, forming the walls of the third ventricle.
Thalamus.clinical: Strokes can cause sensory loss, pain syndromes, or cognitive impairment. Involved in absence seizures and some psychiatric conditions.

Brainstem.summary: The vital connection between brain and spinal cord, controlling life-sustaining functions.
Brainstem.function: Contains nuclei for cranial nerves, regulates breathing and heart rate, controls arousal and sleep-wake cycles. Houses dopamine and serotonin-producing nuclei that project throughout the brain.
Brainstem.location: Between the cerebral hemispheres and spinal cord, anterior to the cerebellum.
Brainstem.clinical: Damage can be life-threatening, affecting breathing, consciousness, and cranial nerve function. Contains critical structures for Parkinson's disease (substantia nigra) and depression (raphe nuclei).

Cerebellum.summary: The coordination center containing more neurons than the rest of the brain combined.
Cerebellum.function: Coordinates movement timing and precision, maintains balance and posture, enables motor learning. Increasingly recognized for roles in cognition, language, and emotional processing.
Cerebellum.location: Posterior cranial fossa, beneath the occipital lobes, behind the brainstem.
Cerebellum.clinical: Damage causes cerebellar ataxia with uncoordinated movements, intention tremor, slurred speech, and balance problems. Can be affected by stroke, MS, or alcohol abuse.

Frontal_Motor.summary: The motor strip and associated areas controlling voluntary movement execution.
Frontal_Motor.function: Primary motor cortex executes movements via direct connections to spinal cord. Supplementary motor area plans complex sequences. Premotor areas prepare movements based on sensory cues.
Frontal_Motor.location: Precentral gyrus and adjacent medial and lateral areas.
Frontal_Motor.clinical: Damage causes contralateral weakness or paralysis. Lesions in supplementary motor area can cause difficulty initiating movements.

Frontal_Prefrontal.summary: The seat of executive function, working memory, and cognitive control.
Frontal_Prefrontal.function: Dorsolateral prefrontal cortex supports working memory and cognitive flexibility. Ventrolateral regions involved in response inhibition. Medial regions support self-reflection and social cognition.
Frontal_Prefrontal.location: Anterior frontal lobe, in front of the motor areas.
Frontal_Prefrontal.clinical: Damage impairs planning, decision-making, and personality. Target for TMS treatment in depression. Abnormal function in ADHD and schizophrenia.

Frontal_Orbital.summary: The ventral frontal surface involved in reward processing and emotional decision-making.
Frontal_Orbital.function: Integrates emotional and reward information into decision-making. Involved in impulse control, social behavior, and representing the value of choices.
Frontal_Orbital.location: Inferior surface of the frontal lobe, above the orbits of the eyes.
Frontal_Orbital.clinical: Damage causes disinhibition, poor social judgment, and impulsive behavior. Involved in addiction and eating disorders.

Parietal_Somatosensory.summary: The primary receiving area for touch and body position sense.
Parietal_Somatosensory.function: Receives and processes tactile information from the body in a somatotopic map. Integrates touch, pressure, temperature, and proprioception.
Parietal_Somatosensory.location: Postcentral gyrus, immediately behind the central sulcus.
Parietal_Somatosensory.clinical: Damage causes numbness and difficulty recognizing objects by touch (astereognosis). Sensory homunculus shows disproportionate hand and face representation.

Parietal_Association.summary: Higher-order areas integrating sensory information for spatial awareness and attention.
Parietal_Association.function: Superior parietal regions process spatial relationships and guide movements. Inferior regions (angular and supramarginal gyri) involved in language, mathematics, and body schema.
Parietal_Association.location: Posterior parietal cortex, behind the postcentral gyrus.
Parietal_Association.clinical: Damage can cause spatial neglect, apraxia (difficulty with skilled movements), and Gerstmann syndrome (writing, calculation, finger recognition deficits).

Temporal_Superior.summary: The auditory processing region containing primary auditory cortex and language areas.
Temporal_Superior.function: Heschl's gyrus is primary auditory cortex. Surrounding areas process complex sounds. Left hemisphere contains Wernicke's area for language comprehension.
Temporal_Superior.location: Superior temporal gyrus, forming the floor of the lateral fissure.
Temporal_Superior.clinical: Damage causes auditory processing deficits. Left-sided damage can cause Wernicke's aphasia with fluent but meaningless speech.

Temporal_Middle_Inferior.summary: Visual object recognition and semantic processing regions.
Temporal_Middle_Inferior.function: Part of the ventral visual stream. Middle temporal gyrus involved in semantic processing. Inferior temporal and fusiform gyri recognize objects, faces, and words.
Temporal_Middle_Inferior.location: Lateral and inferior surfaces of the temporal lobe.
Temporal_Middle_Inferior.clinical: Damage can cause semantic dementia, prosopagnosia (face blindness), or visual agnosia. Fusiform face area damage specifically impairs face recognition.

Temporal_Poles.summary: The anterior tips of the temporal lobes involved in semantic memory and social cognition.
Temporal_Poles.function: Store semantic knowledge and support social and emotional processing. Involved in naming and understanding concepts, especially for unique entities like famous people.
Temporal_Poles.location: Most anterior portion of the temporal lobe.
Temporal_Poles.clinical: Atrophy causes semantic dementia with loss of word and object knowledge. Involved in behavioral variant frontotemporal dementia.

Occipital_Primary.summary: The first cortical stage of visual processing, receiving direct input from the eyes.
Occipital_Primary.function: Calcarine cortex contains V1, which processes basic visual features like orientation and contrast. Cuneus and lingual gyrus contain V2 and V3 for further processing.
Occipital_Primary.location: Medial occipital surface around the calcarine sulcus.
Occipital_Primary.clinical: Damage causes cortical blindness in the corresponding visual field. Bilateral damage can cause complete cortical blindness with potential for 'blindsight'.

Occipital_Association.summary: Higher visual areas processing motion, color, and object features.
Occipital_Association.function: Contains V3, V4, V5/MT and other visual areas. Processes specific features like color (V4) and motion (V5). Feeds into dorsal and ventral visual streams.
Occipital_Association.clinical: Damage can cause selective deficits like motion blindness (akinetopsia) or color processing problems (cerebral achromatopsia).

Cingulate_Anterior.summary: The cognitive and emotional control center of the cingulate cortex.
Cingulate_Anterior.function: Monitors errors and conflicts, regulates emotional responses, drives motivation and goal-directed behavior. Subgenual region involved in mood regulation.
Cingulate_Anterior.location: Anterior portion of the cingulate gyrus, surrounding the genu of the corpus callosum.
Cingulate_Anterior.clinical: Hyperactivity linked to anxiety and OCD. Hypoactivity linked to apathy. Target for deep brain stimulation in treatment-resistant depression.

Cingulate_Mid_Post.summary: Regions involved in attention, memory retrieval, and self-reflection.
Cingulate_Mid_Post.function: Middle cingulate involved in motor planning and pain processing. Posterior cingulate is a hub of the default mode network, active during self-reflection and memory retrieval.
Cingulate_Mid_Post.location: Middle and posterior portions of the cingulate gyrus.
Cingulate_Mid_Post.clinical: Posterior cingulate dysfunction seen in Alzheimer's disease. Middle cingulate involved in chronic pain conditions.

Hippocampal_Formation.summary: The memory encoding center converting experiences into lasting memories.
Hippocampal_Formation.function: Hippocampus proper encodes episodic and spatial memories. Parahippocampal cortex processes scenes and contexts. Together they form the medial temporal memory system.
Hippocampal_Formation.location: Medial temporal lobe, along the floor of the temporal horn of the lateral ventricle.
Hippocampal_Formation.clinical: Damage causes anterograde amnesia. First region affected in Alzheimer's disease. Can generate new neurons throughout life (neurogenesis).

Amygdala_Complex.summary: The emotional significance detector, especially for fear and threat.
Amygdala_Complex.function: Rapidly evaluates stimuli for emotional significance. Modulates memory consolidation for emotional events. Involved in fear learning and social behavior.
Amygdala_Complex.location: Anterior medial temporal lobe, in front of the hippocampus.
Amygdala_Complex.clinical: Hyperactivity linked to anxiety and PTSD. Damage impairs fear recognition and emotional memory. Involved in autism and psychopathy.

Striatum.summary: The input nuclei of the basal ganglia, receiving cortical projections.
Striatum.function: Caudate nucleus involved in cognitive functions and goal-directed behavior. Putamen more involved in motor control and habit learning. Together they gate cortical signals.
Striatum.location: Deep in the cerebral hemispheres, lateral to the internal capsule.
Striatum.clinical: Caudate atrophy characteristic of Huntington's disease. Putamen affected in Parkinson's disease. Both involved in addiction circuitry.

Pallidum_Accumbens.summary: Output and reward nuclei of the basal ganglia system.
Pallidum_Accumbens.function: Globus pallidus provides major output of basal ganglia to thalamus. Nucleus accumbens is central to reward and motivation, receiving dopamine from VTA.
Pallidum_Accumbens.location: Medial to the putamen. Accumbens at the junction of caudate and putamen.
Pallidum_Accumbens.clinical: Pallidum is target for deep brain stimulation in movement disorders. Accumbens central to addiction and depression pathophysiology.

Thalamus_Anterior.summary: Limbic thalamic nuclei involved in memory and emotion.
Thalamus_Anterior.function: Anterior nuclei part of Papez circuit for memory. Ventroanterior nucleus relays basal ganglia output to frontal cortex for motor planning.
Thalamus_Anterior.clinical: Damage can cause memory impairment. Part of circuits affected in Korsakoff syndrome.

Thalamus_Lateral.summary: Sensory and motor relay nuclei of the thalamus.
Thalamus_Lateral.function: Ventral lateral nucleus relays motor information from cerebellum and basal ganglia. VPL relays somatosensory information. LGN relays visual information to occipital cortex.
Thalamus_Lateral.clinical: Strokes can cause contralateral sensory loss or thalamic pain syndrome. LGN lesions cause visual field defects.

Thalamus_Medial.summary: Nuclei involved in cognition, attention, and arousal.
Thalamus_Medial.function: Mediodorsal nucleus connects with prefrontal cortex for executive function. Intralaminar nuclei involved in arousal and attention. MGN relays auditory information.
Thalamus_Medial.clinical: Mediodorsal damage can cause cognitive impairment and personality changes. Intralaminar lesions can affect consciousness.

Thalamus_Pulvinar.summary: The largest thalamic nucleus, involved in visual attention and integration.
Thalamus_Pulvinar.function: Integrates visual, auditory, and somatosensory information. Critical for visual attention and filtering relevant information. Connects extensively with association cortex.
Thalamus_Pulvinar.clinical: Damage can cause visual attention deficits and difficulty filtering distractions.

Midbrain_Nuclei.summary: Dopaminergic and motor control nuclei of the midbrain.
Midbrain_Nuclei.function: Substantia nigra produces dopamine for movement control. VTA produces dopamine for reward and motivation. Red nucleus involved in motor coordination.
Midbrain_Nuclei.clinical: Substantia nigra degeneration causes Parkinson's disease. VTA dysfunction linked to depression and addiction. Locus coeruleus involved in arousal and attention disorders.

Brainstem_Raphe.summary: Serotonin-producing nuclei projecting throughout the brain.
Brainstem_Raphe.function: Major source of serotonin in the brain. Dorsal raphe projects to cortex and limbic system. Median raphe projects to hippocampus. Regulate mood, sleep, and appetite.
Brainstem_Raphe.location: Midline of the brainstem, from midbrain through pons.
Brainstem_Raphe.clinical: Target of antidepressant medications. Dysfunction linked to depression, anxiety, and sleep disorders.

Cerebellum_Hemispheres.summary: The lateral cerebellar regions coordinating limb movements.
Cerebellum_Hemispheres.function: Receive input from cerebral cortex via pons. Coordinate timing and precision of limb movements. Involved in motor learning and cognitive functions through connections with prefrontal cortex.
Cerebellum_Hemispheres.clinical: Damage causes ipsilateral limb ataxia with uncoordinated reaching and intention tremor. Can also affect cognition and language.

Cerebellum_Vermis.summary: The midline cerebellar structure controlling posture and balance.
Cerebellum_Vermis.function: Receives vestibular and spinal input. Controls axial muscles for posture and balance. Coordinates eye movements and gait.
Cerebellum_Vermis.location: Midline of the cerebellum, between the hemispheres.
Cerebellum_Vermis.clinical: Damage causes truncal ataxia with difficulty sitting and standing, wide-based gait, and eye movement abnormalities.
